fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a category with zero morphisms. Then the kernel of a morphism A\xrightarrow {f} B in \mathcal {A} is defined to be a morphism K\xrightarrow {k}{A} such that for any X\xrightarrow {h}A with h;f=0, there exists a unique map X\xrightarrow {}K factoring X\xrightarrow {h}A through K\xrightarrow {k}{A} in the sense of the diagram below:
Evidently, the kernel of a morphism A\xrightarrow {f} B in a category with zero morphisms is the equalizer of A\xrightarrow {f} B with the zero morphism A\xrightarrow {0} B.
We will often write \operatorname {ker}{f}\xrightarrow {i_{\operatorname {ker}{f}}}A for the kernel of a map A\xrightarrow {f}B in a category with zero morphisms \mathcal {A}.

Let \cdots \xrightarrow {}{A}\xrightarrow {f}{B}\xrightarrow {g}{C}\xrightarrow {}\cdots be a sequence in an abelian category \mathcal {A}. The term B is called complex when f;g=0, and exact when the kernel of g is isomorphic to the image of f, in other words \operatorname {ker}{g}=\operatorname {im}{f}.

A category with zero morphisms is defined to be a category enriched in the category of pointed sets and strict maps. The distinguished point of \mathbf {hom}_{\mathcal {A}}{\mathopen {}\left (A,B\right )\mathclose {}} shall be written 0.
